was stated the slaughter of the heifer by a non-priest: Rabbi Ami said: valid. And Rabbi Yitzḥak Nappaḥa said: It is invalid. Ulla said: It is valid, some say It is invalid.
Rabbi Yehoshua bar Abba raised an objection providing a support Rav, I have only the sprinkling of waters, that they are not valid by a woman as when by a man, and that is valid only during the day.

From where to include Its slaughter; and collection of its blood; and sprinkling of its blood; and its burning; and casting of cedar wood, and hyssop, and strip of crimson The verse states: “Law” might I should include even the collection of its ashes, and the filling of waters, and the sanctification. the verse states: this

And what did you see to include these and to exclude those? After that the verse includes and excludes you should say Indeed, we learn all from the sprinkling of its waters: Just as the sprinkling of its waters is not valid by a woman when by a man, and it is valid only during the day, so too I will include its slaughter; and the collection of its blood; and the sprinkling of its blood; and its burning; and casting of cedar wood, and hyssop, and strip of crimson.

Since these are not valid by a woman when by a man, they are valid only during the day. And I exclude the collection of its ashes, and the filling of water, and the sanctification, since these are valid by a woman as when by a man, are valid both during the day and during the night.

And what is the challenge from this If we say that that are invalid by a woman they are also invalid by a non-priest, let sprinkling the waters serve as a proof because invalid by a woman and valid by a non-priest.
Abaye said: This is the challenge: a woman, what is the reason “Elazar,” and not a woman. also a non-priest: “Elazar,” and not a non-priest.
Ulla said: the entire passage implied preclude implied implied on their own
“And you shall give it to Elazar the priest that to Elazar, generations not to Elazar, Some say: In generations, by High Priest. And some say: In generations, by a common priest.

Granted, according to the one who says in generations by a common priest, well, But according to the one who says in generations by the High Priest, from where He derives from Yom Kippur “statute” “statute”

“And he shall take it out he should not take out another with it. As we learned the heifer did not want to go out we do not take out a black with it so that people will not say that they slaughtered the black And we do not take out red with it, so that people will not say they slaughtered two Rabbi says: This is not for reason, rather it is due to that it is stated “It,”
